name: Black hole information paradox
summary: Hawking radiation, computed semi-classically, is exactly thermal — independent of the matter that fell in. Taken at face value, this means information about the infalling state is destroyed when the black hole evaporates, in contradiction with quantum-mechanical unitarity. The puzzle is now widely viewed as a constraint on quantum gravity rather than evidence against unitarity, and recent work on the Page curve in semi-classical gravity has provided a computational handle without resolving the underlying microphysics.

characteristic_numbers.expected: no first-principles QM derivation outside specific contexts
characteristic_numbers.note: BH entropy / area law is the central quantitative target. String theory (Strominger-Vafa 1996) reproduces it for extremal BPS black holes; the generic case is open.

characteristic_numbers.expected: decreasing entanglement entropy at this time required for unitarity
characteristic_numbers.note: Until the Page time, von Neumann entropy of Hawking radiation rises; after, it must decrease for unitarity to hold. Recent work (Penington 2019; Almheiri et al. 2019) reproduces the Page curve from semi-classical gravity with quantum-extremal surfaces.

principles_stressed.note: Strict thermality of Hawking radiation would break QM unitarity. Almost all current research assumes unitarity holds and looks for the missing physics (firewalls, soft hair, replica wormholes, ER=EPR).
Recovering information at infinity requires non-local effects of some form (e.g. ER=EPR, replica wormholes), in tension with low-energy local effective field theory.

history: Hawking (1975) computed thermal radiation, sharpening the puzzle. Page (1993) formulated the entropy-curve constraint for unitary evolution. Strominger-Vafa (1996) reproduced S_BH for extremal BPS black holes from string-theory state counting. Replica-wormhole / quantum-extremal-surface program (Penington 2019; Almheiri-Engelhardt-Marolf-Maxfield 2019) reproduces the Page curve in JT gravity. Microscopic resolution for non-extremal black holes remains open.

references.citation: Hawking, 'Particle Creation by Black Holes', Comm. Math. Phys. 43, 199 (1975)
Page, 'Information in black hole radiation', Phys. Rev. Lett. 71, 3743 (1993)
